entry.title: How to Find the Right Enhancement Spot in Fiori App F3516: A Practical Validation Scenario
entry.content:
Introduction

Validations are a common requirement in SAP Fiori applications. However, identifying the correct enhancement spot for a given app can be challenging.

The Challenge

In this scenario, a validation was required in the Fiori app Manage Profit Centers (Version 2) - F3516 to ensure that the segment value followed specific business rules.

The main challenge was not implementing the validation itself, but identifying the correct enhancement point in the backend.

How the Enhancement Spot Was Identified

A practical approach I often use is to identify the technical package behind the Fiori application.

This can be done by navigating to the Fiori Apps Library, checking the Implementation Information section, and identifying the OData service used by the application.

From there, the OData service can be searched in the ABAP environment (Eclipse), allowing identification of the corresponding package.

In this case, within the application package ODATA_PROFIT_CENTER_V2, an enhancement package with a very intuitive name was found:

ODATA_PROFIT_CENTER_V2_ENH (Enhancements for Manage Profit Center)

CLASS CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX

Within this package, it was possible to identify available enhancement implementations. Among the options, the following example class was used as a reference:

class CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX definition
  public
  final
  create public.

public section.

  interfaces IF_BADI_INTERFACE.
  interfaces IF_BADI_FIN_PC_VALIDATION.

protected section.
private section.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX IMPLEMENTATION.

  method IF_BADI_FIN_PC_VALIDATION~EXECUTE.

    data: ls_validationmessage like line of ct_messages.

    ls_validationmessage-msgid = 'FAC_MPC_PCTR'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgno = '022'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgty = 'E'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv1 = 'The Field '.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv2 = 'Profit Center Category '.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv3 = 'contains invalid value '.

    insert ls_validationmessage into table ct_messages.

  endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

Implementation

The validation logic was implemented to ensure that segment values starting with "0" are not allowed:

METHOD if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute.
    CONSTANTS: BEGIN OF segment_values,
                 geral   TYPE fb_segment VALUE `GERAL`,
                 ato     TYPE fb_segment VALUE `ATO`,
                 nao_ato TYPE fb_segment VALUE `NAO_ATO`,
               END OF segment_values.

    CHECK is_profit_center-profitcenterforedit+09(01) CA `012`.

    DATA(segment) = SWITCH fb_segment( is_profit_center-profitcenterforedit+09(01) WHEN `0` THEN segment_values-geral
                                                                                   WHEN `1` THEN segment_values-ato
                                                                                   WHEN `2` THEN segment_values-nao_ato ).

    IF is_profit_center-segment <> segment.
      INSERT VALUE #( msgid = `ZFIN`
                      msgty = `E`
                      msgno = 001
                      msgv1 = segment ) INTO TABLE ct_messages.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

This validation logic was implemented within the BAdI execution method, ensuring that the business rule is enforced during the save process.

Result

When the validation is triggered, the system prevents saving and displays an error message to the user.

Fiori error message

Key Insight

The most critical part of this implementation was identifying the correct enhancement point.

Once the correct spot was found, the validation itself was straightforward.

This reinforces that, in many SAP extensibility scenarios, the main challenge is not writing the logic itself, but navigating the framework to identify where that logic should be implemented.

Final Thoughts

This scenario highlights that, in many cases, the real challenge is not the implementation itself, but understanding where and how to implement it within the SAP framework.

👉I would be interested to hear how others approach similar challenges.

entry.title: From SEGW to @OData.publish: Expose Your CDS View as OData in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction:

This Blog2 is continuation of blog 1, refer below link:
Link to blog1 
 
In Blog1, we created a CDS view using tables Sales Order Header (VBAK) and Sales Order Items (VBAP). Now, in this Blog, we will make this data available for outside world. 
In S/4HANA, CDS views are not just used for reporting - they can also be exposed as OData service, which allows them to be consumed by external systems, SAP Fiori applications, API and integrations.
 
As an ABAP developer I have always used transaction code SEGW to manually create OData services. But now with CDS, this process is much simpler and faster.

 

Lets see how to expose CDS view as OData : Step by Step:

Step 1: Add OData Annotation

Open your CDS view and add anootation @odata.publish: true
Example below
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZSALEORDREP'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Creating my 1st CDS view'
@OData.publish: true

define view ZCDS_ORDVIEW
    as select from vbak as Header
  association [1..*] to vbap as Item
    on Header.vbeln = Item.vbeln
{
  key Header.vbeln as SalesOrder,
      Header.erdat as CreationDate,
      Header.netwr as NetAmount,
      Header.waerk as Currency,

      case Header.auart
        when '00' then 'Standard Order'
        else 'Other Order Type'
      end as OrderTypeDescription,

      Item
}

Step 2: Activate the CDS View

Press ctrl + F3 or icon activate to activate the CDS view. Once activated, SAP will generate the OData service with name <cdsviewname>_CDS.

In our example OData service name generated is ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS .

Step 3: Activate the Service in SAP Gateway

Even though the service is generated, it must be activated in gateway system. Login to your gateway system and follow below steps:

  1. Go to transaction /n/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  2. Click Add Service
  3. Enter system alias as: your system name
  4. Search service: ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS .
  5. Once its found, select the service and click button 'Add selected Services'.
  6. Assign a package. In my case I used local package $TMP.

 

 
 

Step 4: Test the OData Service

To test the OData service, in gateway system run transaction code /n/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE then it will list down all the services name under technical service name. Select your service ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS and click button SAP Client Gateway.  This will open new screen SAP gateway client. Refer below screenshots:
 
 

$metadata shows full structure like Fields, types, associations

Example: Use the following URL to filter a specific sales order: /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$filter=SalesOrder eq '00XXXXX178' Note: Ensure there are no spaces around the '=' in the $filter parameter.


 /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW/$count

You can also use different OData query options like Top 5 records: ...?$top=5, Sort data: ...?$orderby=CreationDate desc etc. These options help in optimizing data retrieval and are widely used in Fiori applications.

To test using URL, use below format. For testing I have used Postman :

https://<host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$top=5

 
The OData service can also be tested outside of SAP using tools like: - Browser,  Postman or Curl Example: https://<host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$top=5

When you add @OData.publish: true:

  • SAP automatically creates an OData service
  • Associations become navigation properties
  • CDS fields are exposed as OData properties
  • No manual service modeling is required

This is why CDS is preferred in modern development.

Conclusion

Exposing CDS view as an OData service shows how simple and powerful modern ABAP development has become. With just a single annotation, you can turn your data model into a consumable service ready for applications, APIs, and integrations. What once required manual effort in SEGW can now be achieved in minutes using CDS. This not only improves productivity but also ensures consistency between your data model and service layer.

What’s Next?

In the next step, we’ll enhance this CDS view with UI annotations and generate a Fiori application without writing frontend code.

Let’s Discuss

Have you started exposing CDS views as OData services?

Are you still using SEGW in your projects?

Share your thoughts and experiences.

entry.title: Principal Propagation from BTP Web App to S/4HANA via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

During a recent customer PoC, I worked on a scenario where a BTP-hosted web application needed to call an S/4HANA on-premise API — not as a generic service account, but as the actual logged-in user. 
This is where Principal Propagation becomes essential. Preserving user identity end-to-end isn't just a technical nicety — it matters for auditability, compliance, and access control. When S/4HANA receives a call as the real user, every action is traceable: security audit logs show who did what, authorizations are enforced per user, and there's no shared service account masking individual activity.

In this post, I share the configuration and lessons learned that made it work—from a BTP web application authenticated via SAP Cloud Identity Services, through SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Connector, all the way to S/4HANA, with user identity preserved throughout the entire chain.

Thanks to @cj_lo  for preparing Cloud Connector with S/HANA On-premise demo system, testing, and troubleshoot together.

Scenario

A simple web application is deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. When the user clicks a button, it triggers an iFlow on SAP Integration Suite that fetches data from S/4HANA. The S/4HANA system must receive the call as the authenticated user — not as a technical service account. 
While direct access to S/4HANA via a BTP Destination and SAP Cloud Connector is possible, many organizations choose to introduce a middleware layer to support centralized governance, security, monitoring, and error handling. Ultimately, the target architecture is customer-specific and should balance integration requirements, operational considerations, governance needs, and cost.

Browser (logged-in user)
  → BTP Web App (via Approuter + Cloud Identity Services)
    → BTP Destination Service
      → SAP Integration Suite iFlow
        → Cloud Connector
          → S/4HANA (on-premise, as the user)

Prerequisites & Scope

This article assumes the following components are already installed and configured:

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Connector connected to the SAP BTP subaccount
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the Identity Provider
  • SAP S/4HANA system accessible through Cloud Connector
  • User identities mapped between IAS and SAP S/4HANA

This article focuses on the principal propagation scenario from a SAP BTP application through SAP Integration Suite to an on-premise SAP S/4HANA system. Topics such as SAP Cloud Connector installation, SAP BTP onboarding and trust configuration, access control setup, certificate management, and SAP S/4HANA user provisioning are outside the scope of this article. If I were to cover all of them here, I would end up writing a book instead of a mini blog 😉

Relevant SAP documentation is listed in the References section at the end of this article.

Solution Overview

The solution relies on three key components working together:

ComponentRole
ApprouterHandles IAS login, holds the user's JWT token
BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)Exchanges the user's IAS JWT for an Integration Suite-scoped token using the PI Runtime service key
iFlowReceives the user-scoped token, calls S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation

The critical insight is using OAuth2JWTBearer as the destination authentication type. This is the only type that correctly takes the user's IAS JWT and exchanges it at the PI Runtime token endpoint — preserving the user identity all the way to SAP Integration Suite.

Configuration

The BTP app consists of the following files — each is covered in the steps below:

your-app/
├── manifest.yml
├── package.json
├── xs-app.json
├── xs-security-ias.json
└── dist/
    └── index.html

Provision BTP Cloud Foundry Services

Before deploying the app, create the required service instances in your Cloud Foundry (CF) space.
Below using CF CLI, if you need a crash course using it, please check out this tutorial here.

Target your CF API endpoint and log in (in this example my region is jp10, yours could be different):

cf api https://api.cf.jp10.hana.ondemand.com
cf login

Create a Destination service instance (used by the Approuter to resolve BTP destinations), you can replace iflow-pp-destination-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service destination lite iflow-pp-destination-svc

Create an IAS (Identity) service instance using the xs-security-ias.json security descriptor, you can replace iflow-ias-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service identity application iflow-pp-ias-svc -c xs-security-ias.json

Where xs-security-ias.json contains:

{
  "xsappname": "iflow-pp-ias",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback?authType=ias"
    ],
    "post-logout-redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/*/logout.html"
    ]
  }
}

credential-type: X509_GENERATED uses a certificate-based credential instead of a client secret — recommended for production.

Both service instances are bound to the app via manifest.yml:

applications:
  - name: poc-iflow-pp-fe
    memory: 512M
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    services:
      - iflow-pp-ias-svc
      - iflow-pp-destination-svc

Ensure the User's Email Matches S/4HANA Identity

The Cloud Connector Subject Pattern (configured under Cloud To On-Premise → Principal Propagation) determines how the X.509 certificate CN is built. The default pattern is:

CN=${name}

${name} is sourced from the user_name claim in the PI Runtime token. During the OAuth2JWTBearer exchange, PI Runtime populates user_name from the email claim of the incoming IAS JWT — not from sub or any other claim.

So the identity that reaches Cloud Connector and ultimately S/4HANA is always the user's email address.
This behaviour is important because many troubleshooting efforts focus on the IAS sub claim, while principal propagation in this flow ultimately depends on the user's email address being propagated and resolvable by the backend system.

Important: Make sure the user's email address in IAS matches a user identity that S/4HANA can resolve — either via direct email-to-username mapping on the S/4 side, or as the logon name if S/4 uses email as the logon name format.
Depending on your identity governance requirements, the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern can use mapping logic beyond a basic CN. On the S/4HANA side, certificate-to-user mapping (CERTRULE) adds a second layer of control, allowing a certificate CN to be mapped to a different logon name. For complex scenarios, refer to the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern Syntax and S/4HANA Certificate-Based User Mapping documentation to design patterns that fit your enterprise architecture.

To verify what identity Cloud Connector is propagating, check CC Admin UI → Logging and Tracing — the outbound request will show the CN value used in the short-lived certificate.

Here's how my configuration looks like in Cloud Connector:

 Here's how rule-based certificate mapping looks like in S/4HANA - /ncertrule

Create Process Integration Runtime Service Instance

You probably has setup this one in order to trigger any published iFlow; or if you have one but has not included below grant type, then you can create a new one in BTP Cockpit → Service Marketplace → Process Integration Runtime:

  • Plan: integration-flow
  • Grant Types to enable:
    • client_credentials (for testing)
    • jwt bearer (the one that we need)
    • refresh_token
    • saml2 bearer
  • Create a Service Key and note down clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl

Why PI Runtime and not IAS credentials? In my landscape setup, SAP Integration Suite only trusts tokens issued by its own OAuth authorization server (via the PI Runtime instance - current the SAP Integration Suite is authenticated via SAP Default idP). IAS tokens forwarded directly are not trusted by SAP Integration Suite unless full IAS-to-Integration Suite trust federation is set up. The PI Runtime service key is the standard supported path.

Configure BTP Destination

In BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

FieldValue
Name<unique destination name, i.e. m25_iflow>
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://<your-cpi-host>/http/<your-iflow-endpoint>
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2JWTBearer
Client ID<clientid from PI Runtime service key>
Client Secret<clientsecret from PI Runtime service key>
Token Service URL<tokenurl from PI Runtime service key>

No additional properties required.

Common pitfalls:

  • Do not use OAuth2UserTokenExchange — it uses refresh_token grant internally which PI Runtime does not support
  • Do not use IAS credentials here — SAP Integration Suite will reject them
  • The destination name must exactly match what is referenced in xs-app.json (watch for underscore vs hyphen)

BTP App — Approuter Setup

The frontend app is a plain HTML file served by the SAP Approuter (@sap/approuter). No custom middleware needed.

package.json:

{
  "name": "ar-iflow-pp-fe",
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/approuter": "^22.0.3"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node node_modules/@sap/approuter/approuter.js"
  }
}

xs-app.json — routes inbound requests to the BTP destination or local static files:
Also remember to double-check the Destination name here in case you're using different name.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "logout": { "logoutEndpoint": "/logout" },
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/user-api(.*)",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "sap-approuter-userapi"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/m25_iflow(.*)$",
      "destination": "m25_iflow",
      "csrfProtection": false,
      "target": "$1",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/(.*)$",
      "localDir": "dist",
      "authenticationType": "ias",
      "cacheControl": "no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate"
    }
  ]
}

The /user-api route uses the built-in Approuter user info service — no custom code required to read the logged-in user's details.

dist/index.html — the entire frontend in one file:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <title>Principal Propagation PoC</title>
  <style>
    body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 40px auto; padding: 0 16px; }
    button { padding: 8px 20px; margin-top: 8px; cursor: pointer; }
    pre { background: #f4f4f4; padding: 12px; white-space: pre-wrap; word-break: break-all; margin-top: 8px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

<h2>Principal Propagation PoC</h2>

<h3>Logged-in User</h3>
  <pre id="userInfo">Loading...</pre>

<h3>Trigger iFlow</h3>
  <button onclick="callIflow()">Call iFlow (GET /m25_iflow)</button>
  <pre id="iflowResponse">Response will appear here</pre>

<script>
    // Fetch logged-in user info from built-in Approuter user API
    fetch("/user-api/currentUser")
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(u => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = JSON.stringify(u, null, 2))
      .catch(e => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);

// Trigger iFlow via BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)
    function callIflow() {
      call("/m25_iflow");
    }

function call(url) {
      document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Calling " + url + "...";
      fetch(url, {
        headers: { "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest" }
      })
        .then(async r => {
          const text = await r.text();
          try { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(text), null, 2); } catch (_) { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + text; }
        })
        .then(body => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = body)
        .catch(e => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);
    }
  </script>

</body>
</html>

Deploy:

cf push

Assign ESBMessaging.send Role to Users (IAS Group Mapping)

In BTP Cockpit → Security → Role Collections → Create (e.g. CPIMessagingSend) :

Roles tab — add:

Application IdentifierRole TemplateRole
it-rt-<your-instance>ESBMessagingESBMessaging.send

User Groups tab — add:

Identity ProviderName
<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com — Business UsersIntegration_Senders

Make sure your users are members of the Integration_Senders group in IAS, and select Business Users (not Application Users) as the user type — logged-in app users are business users in IAS.

iFlow configuration summary: The iFlow uses an HTTPS Sender Adapter with User Role authorization (ESBMessaging.send) and an OData/HTTP Receiver Adapter configured for On-Premise proxy type with Principal Propagation authentication. See the Integration Flow section below for the full configuration.

Building Integration Flow

This section assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite.

The integration flow in this demo scenario is intentionally minimal — its purpose is to receive the inbound call from the BTP web app, forward it to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation, and return the response.

The iFlow contains the following steps:

  1. HTTPS Sender Adapter — receives the inbound HTTP request from the BTP Approuter
  2. Request-Reply step — forwards the call to the S/4HANA OData service
  3. OData V2 Receiver Adapter — calls S/4HANA on-premise via Cloud Connector

HTTPS Sender Adapter configuration:

FieldValue
Address/your-iflow-endpoint
AuthorizationUser Role
User RoleESBMessaging.send

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Connection tab:

FieldValue
Addresshttp://<virtual-host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>
Proxy TypeOn-Premise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
Location ID<your Cloud Connector location ID>
Custom Query Parametersap-client=<client no.>

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Processing tab:

FieldValue
OperationQuery (GET)
Resource Path<EntitySetName>
Query Options$top=10 (or as needed)
Custom Query Options<SAP Client Number>

The sap-client parameter must go in Custom Query Parameters (Connection tab). The Resource Path must have no leading slash — e.g. `A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`, not `/A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`.

Testing the Flow

With the web app deployed, open it in the browser. You will see the logged-in user's identity displayed (via the /user-api/currentUser endpoint exposed by the Approuter). Click Call via iFlow (GET /m25_iflow) button to trigger the integration flow.

A successful end-to-end flow will show the same user email across the BTP app, the Integration Suite message log, and the S/4HANA audit log. The sections below describe how to verify each layer.

Cloud Connector — Trace

If the call reaches the Cloud Connector but something goes wrong on the S/4HANA side, enable tracing in the Cloud Connector Admin UI:

  1. Go to Cloud Connector Admin UI → Logs and Trace → Trace (Debug level)
  2. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  3. View the trace (scc_core.trc) and search for the CN value — this confirms which user identity Cloud Connector placed in the short-lived X.509 certificate sent to S/4HANA

This is especially useful when the call appears to succeed from the Integration Suite side but S/4HANA returns a 401 or an authorization error — it lets you confirm the CN matches a user S/4HANA can resolve.

S/4HANA — ICF Trace (`/nsectrace`)

For the deepest level of verification, enable ICF tracing on the S/4HANA side to see exactly what the system receives at the HTTP layer:

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /nsectrace
  2. Navigate to ICF Service / Path and enable the trace for the relevant ICF service path (e.g. `/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>`) and click Activate Trace
  3. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  4. Back in /nsectrace, click Deactivate Trace, and choose Show Trace — the trace output will show the inbound request headers including the ssl-client-subject field, which contains the CN from the Cloud Connector certificate

The CN value here is what S/4HANA evaluates for user mapping. If it does not match a known logon name, the call will fail with a 401 even if everything upstream is correct.

Key Takeaways

Principal Propagation is more than a technical configuration—it enables auditability, compliance, and least-privilege access control by ensuring S/4HANA receives requests as the actual business user rather than a shared service account.

The implementation approach depends on customer requirements and the overall solution architecture. Whether user identity is propagated through direct connectivity or a middleware layer, the objective remains the same: maintain end-to-end user context while balancing integration needs, governance, operational considerations, and cost.

One of the key lessons learned is that principal propagation depends on several components being configured consistently across BTP, Integration Suite, Cloud Connector, and S/4HANA. Even small trust or authorization gaps can prevent user identity from being propagated successfully, making end-to-end validation essential.

References

The following SAP documentation was useful while implementing and troubleshooting this scenario.

SAP BTP - Cloud Foundry Environment

  • Administration and Operations in the Cloud Foundry

SAP Cloud Connector and S/4HANA (ABAP)

  • Cloud Connector Documentation
  • Installation Guide
  • Managing Subaccounts
  • Principal Propagation Configuration
  • Configure Access Control (HTTP)
  • Configure Subject Patterns
  • Subject Pattern Rules API
  • S/4HANA / ABAP – Rule-Based Certificate Mapping (CERTRULE)

SAP Integration Suite

  • SAP Integration Suite Documentation
  • Cloud Integration Documentation

SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Identity Authentication (IAS) Documentation
  • Destination Service Documentation
  • OAuth2JWTBearer Authentication

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA の SOAP API を使って仕訳伝票を転記してみた
entry.content:
本記事では、SAP S/4HANA の SOA Manager を使って SOAP API（Journal Entry Create Request Confirmation）を外部からコールできるよう設定し、実際に仕訳伝票を作成するまでの手順を解説します。RESTful API やOData APIが主流になりつつある昨今ですが、既存の EDI 連携や外部システムとのインターフェースには依然として SOAP/Web Service が多く使われています。SAP S/4HANA でも SOA Manager を使えば簡単に公開・利用できます。

 

1. 対象 API の概要

今回使用する API は以下のとおりです。

正式名称

Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous)

Technical Name

JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In

Namespace

http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN

Operation

JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In

用途

外部システムから S/4HANA に仕訳伝票（Journal Entry）を同期的に登録する

このサービスの特徴として、1 回のリクエストで複数の JournalEntryCreateRequest を含めることができるBulk 対応になっています。大量データの投入には非同期版 API（Journal Entry - Post (Asynchronous)）の利用が推奨されています。

仕入先請求書を作成したい場合は、Supplier Invoice - OData V2 や Supplier Invoice - Create (A2X, Inbound, Synchronous) の利用を検討してください。

 

1.1 サービス構造（Service Structure）

リクエストメッセージは MessageHeader ノードと JournalEntryCreateRequest ノードで構成されます。JournalEntry ノードの配下には以下のノードが含まれます。

 

ノード

説明

JournalEntry

会社コード・転記日付など仕訳ヘッダー情報

Item

G/L 勘定科目の明細行

DebtorItem

得意先（売掛金）勘定の明細行

CreditorItem

仕入先（買掛金）勘定の明細行

ProductTaxItem

税勘定の明細行

 

なお、MessageHeader の TestDataIndicator を true に設定すると、実際に転記せずシミュレーション実行が可能です。動作確認時に活用できます。

スキーマや WSDL の詳細は以下の公式リソースで確認できます。

  • SAP API Business Hub - JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In: https://api.sap.com/api/OP_JOURNALENTRYCREATEREQUESTCONF_IN/overview
  • SAP Help Portal - Journal Entry Create Request Confirmation: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/f5c8d0579212c525e10000000a4450e5.html

 

2. 前提条件

  • SAP S/4HANA（本記事では S4H クライアント 400 を使用）
  • SOA Manager（トランザクション: SOAMANAGER）へのアクセス権
  • 仕訳伝票を作成できる FI 権限
  • SOAP クライアント（本記事では Postman または SAP Integration Suite を想定）

 

3. SOA Manager でサービスを公開する

SAP S/4HANA で SOAP API を外部公開するには、SOA Manager でサービス定義に対してバインディングを作成し、エンドポイント URL を払い出す必要があります。以下の手順で設定します。

 

3.1 SOAMANAGER トランザクションを起動する

SAP GUI でトランザクションコード：SOAMANAGER を入力して実行します。ブラウザが起動し、SOA Management の画面が表示されます。

図1: SOAMANAGER トランザクションの起動

 

3.2 Web Service Configuration を開く

SOA Management のトップ画面で、[Service Administration] タブにある「Web Service Configuration」をクリックします。

図2: Web Service Configuration の選択

 

3.3 サービス定義を検索する

Web Service Configuration の検索画面で、以下の条件でサービス定義を検索します。

Object Type

Service Definition

Object Name (contains)

JOURNALENTRYC*

 

「Search」ボタンをクリックすると、「JOURNALENTRYCREATEREQUEСТC...」というサービス定義が見つかります。

図3: JOURNALENTRYCREATEREQUESTCONFI のサービス定義を検索

 

3.4 サービス（バインディング）を作成する

サービス定義のリンクをクリックして詳細画面を開きます。「Configurations」タブを選択し、「Create Service」ボタンをクリックします。

図4: Create Service ボタンのクリック

 

3.5 サービス名とバインディング名を設定する（ステップ 1/4）

新規バインディング作成ウィザードが起動します。ステップ 1「Service and Binding Name」で以下を入力し、「Next」をクリックします。

Service Name

ZTT_sync_JournalEntryCreation

New Binding Name

ZTT_sync_JournalEntryCreation

 

図5: サービス名・バインディング名の設定

名前は任意ですが、後で識別しやすいよう API の用途を反映した名前にするとよいでしょう。

 

3.6 プロバイダーセキュリティを設定する（ステップ 2/4）

ステップ 2「Provider Security」でセキュリティ設定を行います。本記事はあくまで技術検証を目的としているため、設定をできるだけシンプルにしています。実環境では、以下の点を必ず見直してください。

Transport Level Security

None（HTTP）

Message Level Security

None

Authentication Method

User ID / Password（Basic 認証）

 

⚠️  実環境での設定について
本記事の設定は技術検証用です。実環境では以下を適切に設定してください。
・Transport Level Security: SSL（HTTPS）を使用し、通信を暗号化する
・Authentication Method: X.509 クライアント証明書や SAP Assertion Ticket（シングルサインオン）などのより強力な認証機能の利用を検討する
・Message Level Security: 機密性の高いデータを扱う場合は署名・暗号化を検討する
・接続元 IP の制限など、ネットワーク層でのアクセス制御も合わせて実施する

 

図6: Provider Security の設定（Transport Level）

 

図7: Provider Security の設定（Authentication Method）

 

3.7 SOAP Protocol を設定する（ステップ 3/4）

ステップ 3「SOAP Protocol」では Transport Binding の設定を確認します。「Calculated Protocol」が HTTP になっていることを確認し、「Next」をクリックします。特別な要件がない限りデフォルト値のままで問題ありません。

図8: SOAP Protocol の設定

 

3.8 Operation Settings を確認して完了する（ステップ 4/4）

ステップ 4「Operation Settings」では、このバインディングで公開するオペレーションが「JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In」になっていることを確認します。「Finish」ボタンをクリックして設定を完了します。

図9: Operation Settings の確認

 

3.9 サービスが作成されたことを確認する

ウィザードが完了すると、Configurations タブにサービス「ZTT_SYNC_JOURNALENTRYCREATION」とバインディング「ZTT_sync_JournalEntryCreation」が表示されます。State が「Active」になっていれば成功です。

図10: サービス作成完了の確認

 

4. WSDL と SOAP エンドポイント URL を確認する

サービスが作成されたら、SOAP クライアントから接続するために WSDL URL と SOAP エンドポイント URL を取得します。

 

4.1 WSDL URL の取得

作成したバインディングの Actions アイコンから「WSDL Generation」を選択します。「WSDL Flavours」でオプションを選択（デフォルトで問題なし）すると、下部に「WSDL URL for Binding」が表示されます。

図11: WSDL URL の確認

WSDL URL をブラウザで開くと XML 定義が確認できます。Postman や SoapUI にこの URL を貼り付けることでオペレーションが自動的にインポートされます。

 

4.2 SOAP エンドポイント URL の確認

WSDL 内の <wsdl:service> 要素にある location 属性が SOAP エンドポイント URL です。ブラウザで WSDL URL を開き、location を検索するか、以下のパターンで URL が構成されています。

http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/srt/xip/sap/journalentrycreaterequestconfi/400/ztt_sync_journalentrycreation/ztt_sync_journalentrycreation

図12: WSDL 内の SOAP エンドポイント URL（location 属性）

 

5. SOAP リクエストを送信する

取得した WSDL URL を Postman や SoapUI にインポートし、SOAP リクエストを作成・送信します。リクエスト送信時には HTTP ヘッダーに SOAPAction を指定します。なお、リクエスト内の G/L 勘定科目・会社コード・仕入先 ID はあらかじめ S/4HANA に登録済みであることが前提です。

【HTTP ヘッダー】

Content-Type: text/xml; charset=utf-8
SOAPAction: http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN/JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In/JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_InRequest
sap-client: 400

【リクエスト Body（税コードあり・仕入先CreditorItemで締める例）】

<soapenv:Envelope
  xmlns:soapenv="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/"
  xmlns:sfin="http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN">
  <soapenv:Header/>
  <soapenv:Body>
    <sfin:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest>

      <!-- Bulk ヘッダー：複数の JournalEntryCreateRequest をまとめる単位 -->
      <MessageHeader>
        <ID>TEST-BULK-001</ID>
        <CreationDateTime>2026-07-10T10:00:00Z</CreationDateTime>
      </MessageHeader>

      <!-- 1件目の仕訳リクエスト -->
      <JournalEntryCreateRequest>
        <MessageHeader>
          <ID>TEST-001</ID>
          <CreationDateTime>2026-07-10T10:00:00Z</CreationDateTime>
        </MessageHeader>
        <JournalEntry>
          <BusinessTransactionType>RFBU</BusinessTransactionType>
          <AccountingDocumentType>SA</AccountingDocumentType>
          <CompanyCode>ZIT1</CompanyCode>
          <DocumentDate>2026-07-10</DocumentDate>
          <PostingDate>2026-07-10</PostingDate>
          <DocumentHeaderText>SOAP API テスト転記</DocumentHeaderText>

          <!-- 借方: 費用 G/L（税抜金額） -->
          <Item>
            <GLAccount listID="INT">66999981</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="JPY">5000</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DebitCreditCode>S</DebitCreditCode>
            <Tax><TaxCode>V3</TaxCode></Tax>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <CostCenter>ZIT1_CC01</CostCenter>
              <ProfitCenter>YB900</ProfitCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>

          <!-- 消費税明細（ProductTaxItem） -->
          <ProductTaxItem>
            <TaxCode>V3</TaxCode>
            <ConditionType>MWVR</ConditionType>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="JPY">500</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <TaxBaseAmountInTransCrcy currencyCode="JPY">5000</TaxBaseAmountInTransCrcy>
          </ProductTaxItem>

          <!-- 貸方: 仕入先勘定（税込合計 = 税抜 + 消費税） -->
          <CreditorItem>
            <ReferenceDocumentItem>1</ReferenceDocumentItem>
            <Creditor>ZIT_BP03</Creditor>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="JPY">-5500</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DebitCreditCode>H</DebitCreditCode>
          </CreditorItem>

        </JournalEntry>
      </JournalEntryCreateRequest>

    </sfin:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest>
  </soapenv:Body>
</soapenv:Envelope>

TestDataIndicator を true にするとシミュレーション実行になり、実際の転記は行われません。各 JournalEntryCreateRequest の MessageHeader に <TestDataIndicator>true</TestDataIndicator> を追加することで有効になります。初めて試す際はまず true で動作を確認することをお勧めします。

 

5.1 レスポンスの確認ポイント

正常処理時は HTTP 200 が返り、レスポンス Body に JournalEntryCreateConfirmation が含まれます。以下の項目で成否を確認してください。

レスポンス要素

内容

AccountingDocument

作成された伝票番号（転記失敗時は 0）

CompanyCode

会社コード

Log / MaximumLogItemSeverityCode

最大重要度コード（E=エラー、W=警告、S=成功）

 

5.2 主なエラーコードと対処方法

転記が失敗した場合、Log の Note 要素にエラーメッセージが返ります。よくあるエラーと対処方法を以下に示します。

エラーメッセージ（抜粋）

対処方法

D/C code doesn't match the amount sign (FINS_FI_POSTING_IF 047)

DebitCreditCode と金額の符号を確認。マイナス転記の場合は全明細の符号を揃える。

G/L account & requires a valid tax code (FS 214)

税コード必須の G/L 勘定を使用している。対象勘定のマスターを確認するか税コードを指定する。

For tax code &1, the key date &2 does not fall in any validity period (FF 825)

ヘッダーに <TaxDeterminationDate> を指定して有効期限内の日付を設定する。

Negative postings not allowed (FINS_FI_POSTING_IF056)

会社コードと伝票タイプでマイナス転記が許可されているか Customizing を確認する。

 

本ブログにおいては、認証は Basic 認証（User ID / Password）で行なっています。Postman の場合は Authorization タブで Type を「Basic Auth」に設定し、SAP のユーザー ID とパスワードを入力します。なお、リクエスト内の G/L 勘定科目（GLAccount）・会社コード（CompanyCode）はあらかじめ SAP S/4HANA に登録済みであることが前提です。未登録のマスターデータを指定した場合はエラーレスポンスが返ります。

⚠️  実環境での認証について
本記事では検証の簡便さを優先して Basic 認証を使用していますが、本番環境では以下を推奨します。
・通信路は必ず HTTPS（SSL）で保護する
・可能であれば X.509 クライアント証明書や SAP Assertion Ticket（SSO）を利用する
・呼び出しに使用するユーザーは、必要最小限の権限（FI 転記権限のみ）に絞った専用のシステムユーザーを割り当てる

 

6. 結果を確認する

SOAP リクエストが正常に処理されると、HTTP 200 のレスポンスが返ります。SAP 側では FBL3N（G/L Account Line Item Display）などで仕訳が転記されたことを確認できます。

下図は、SOAP API 経由で作成した仕訳伝票を FBL3N で確認した結果です。G/L Account 66999981 に対して、転記が行われています。

図13: FBL3N で確認した仕訳伝票

 

7. まとめ

本記事では、SAP S/4HANA の SOA Manager を使って SOAP API を公開し、外部クライアントから仕訳伝票を作成するまでの手順を解説しました。手順をまとめると以下のとおりです。

  1. SOAMANAGER（トランザクション）を起動する
  2. Web Service Configuration でサービス定義「JOURNALENTRYCREATEREQUEST CONFI」を検索する
  3. Create Service ウィザードでバインディングを作成する（名前・セキュリティ・プロトコル・オペレーション）
  4. WSDL URL を取得し、SOAP エンドポイント URL を確認する
  5. SOAP クライアントから Basic 認証付きでリクエストを送信する
  6. FBL3N 等で仕訳の転記結果を確認する

 

SAP S/4HANA には REST ベースの OData API も豊富に用意されていますが、既存の EDI やレガシーシステムとの連携など SOAP が必要なシナリオでも、SOA Manager を活用することでスムーズに対応できます。本記事が皆さんの統合プロジェクトの参考になれば幸いです。

 

参考リンク

  • SAP Business Accelerator Hub - Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous): https://api.sap.com/api/OP_JOURNALENTRYCREATEREQUESTCONF_IN/overview
  • SAP Help Portal - Journal Entry Create Request Confirmation (Service Description): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b978f98fc5884ff2aeb10c8fdeb8a43b/f5c8d0579212c525e10000000a4450e5.html

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Custom ABAP CDS View to Datasphere: Replication Flow Setup, Status & Delta Processing
entry.content:
Introduction: 

This technical guide explains how to replicate data from a custom ABAP CDS view in SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows with Change Data Capture (CDC). It covers the concepts, the end-to-end setup, how deltas are captured, the related jobs and system tables, and what to expect during initial and delta runs.
You’ll learn:
  • How RMS, APE, and CDC work together
  • How to prepare a CDC-enabled custom CDS view
  • How to configure a Replication Flow in Datasphere for Initial only and Initial + Delta loads
  • What artifacts (logging and buffer tables, jobs) are created in S/4HANA and how to monitor them
  • How to validate inserts, updates, and deletes end-to-end
  • SAP Datasphere local table, Internal delta capture columns (Change Date or Change Type).

Basics:

  • Replication Management Service (RMS): Orchestrates change data capture lifecycles, creates and monitors master/subscriber logging tables, and runs real-time jobs for observing and pushing deltas. Manages the end-to-end reliability of triggers and logging.
  • ABAP Pipeline Engine (APE): An ABAP-based runtime used by Datasphere to extract from ABAP sources (S/4HANA tables or CDC-enabled CDS views). Acts as the “consumer” in CDC terms for Replication Flows. Supporting Initial Only, Delta Only & Initial and Delta options.
  • Change Data Capture (CDC): Uses database triggers to record INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE changes on base tables that back an ABAP CDS view.Captured changes flow from: Base table triggers → Master Logging Table → Subscriber Logging Table → Buffer Table → Replication Flow into Datasphere target.

Prerequisites and Preparation:

Authorizations:
  • ABAP development access to create a CDS view
  • Access to SM37, DHADM, DHCDCMON, DHRDBMON for monitoring
  • Permissions in SAP Datasphere to create and run Replication Flows
System readiness:
  • RFC/ODP or ABAP connection from Datasphere to S/4HANA (as per your Datasphere connection setup)
  • Sufficient background work processes for real-time jobs
SAP S/4HANA RMS job settings, scheudling and operational tuning:

Standard jobs (auto-created/managed, visible in SM37; commonly run under DDIC)

  • /1DH/CDC_HEALTH_CHECK: Periodic CDC health verification.
  • SAP_DHCDC_DISPATCHER: Dispatcher for CDC orchestration.
  • /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB: Moves changes from Master Logging Table to Subscriber Logging Table. Default reschedule interval ~1 hour.
  • /1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA (aka /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA): Joins subscriber logs with base table and writes Buffer Table. Default reschedule interval ~6 hours.

Tuning guidance:

  • Start with defaults, then shorten intervals if your delta SLAs require it and your system resources allow.
  • Coordinate with Basis to adjust job class/priority and periodicity to avoid long-run job lists and ensure timely delta movement.
  • Monitor DHCDCMON (log table depth) and DHRDBMON (buffer latency). If backlogs build, increase job frequency and/or background work processes.

Connectivity:

  • ABAP or SAP S/4HANA Connection between SAP Datasphere and SAP S/4HANA.
  • SAP Datasphere + SAP S/4HANA: Your Guide to Seamless Data Integration.
  • SAP Cloud Connector for SAP S/4HANA (Private/ On-prem).
Design recommendations: Prefer SAP standard released CDS views or SAP data products where possible for lifecycle stability. If not then create custom ABAP CDS view/entity, data products instead of using direct ABAP tables as context information is lost and significant effort is required to reverse-engineer the logic. 
  • SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

1. Create a CDC‑Enabled Custom CDS View

The example below shows a simplified custom ABAP CDS view Z_SALESORDER on top of a custom base table ZVBAK. It enables analytical usage and data extraction, and configures CDC mapping so deltas are captured.

Sample reference code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZISDSALESORDER'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@EndUserText.label: 'Z Sales Order'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel: {
   compositionRoot:   true,
   representativeKey: 'SalesOrder',
   usageType: {
     dataClass:      #TRANSACTIONAL,
     serviceQuality: #B,
     sizeCategory:   #L
   },
   supportedCapabilities: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION, #CDS_MODELING_ASSOCIATION_TARGET, #SQL_DATA_SOURCE, #CDS_MODELING_DATA_SOURCE, #EXTRACTION_DATA_SOURCE ],
   modelingPattern: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION ]
}
@ObjectModel.sapObjectNodeType.name: 'SalesOrder'

@Analytics: {
    dataExtraction: {
        enabled: true,
        delta.changeDataCapture: {
            mapping:[
                {
                    table: 'zvbak', role: #MAIN,
                    viewElement: ['SalesOrder'],
                    tableElement: ['vbeln']  }
            ]
        }
    }
 }
define view Z_SALESORDER as select from zvbak
//Association
  association [0..*]...{<Columns>};

For testing, I developed a custom ABAP program to perform CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on the ZVBAK custom table and validate end‑to‑end propagation. I initially inserted 10 records.

Use transaction SE16N on the CDS SQL view ZISDSALESORDER to view the record count.

2. Create a Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere.

Replication Flows are the recommended mechanism for high‑volume replication into SAP Datasphere and supported targets.

2.1 Note: If you choose Initial Only mode, the target will have the same columns as the source (130 columns in this case).

2.2 When using the Initial and Delta mode, SAP Datasphere adds two internal columns (Change_Type and Change_Date) to the Z_SALESORDER local table. The following Change_Type values are typically observed in ABAP CDS replication. Replication Flow Scenarios with Different Load Types.

OperationChange_Type
Initial Load L
Pure Insert I
Delta Load (UPSERT: insert if not exists, else update by key )A 
Delta Load (DELETE)D 

Note: The delta capture table is an internal SAP Datasphere object whose structure may change at any time and is not released for external access.It is only used by SAP Datasphere internal applications (for example, Transformation Flows, Replication Flows etc.)Using the internal delta capture columns (Change_Date or Change_Type) or their content for any external delta replication outside Premium Outbound Integration is not permitted. 

3725855 - Delta Columns CHANGE_TYPE and CHANGE_DATE not visible in Data Viewer or Data Products for Local Tables in HDLF Spaces in SAP Datasphere

3634750 - Unexpected behavior with delta enabled Datasphere local table and models using the same feature

2.3  The Replication Flow’s delta frequency is configured for every 5 minutes (default: 1 hour). You can modify this as required. Using 0 hours and 0 minutes switches to near real‑time.

 

3. Execute the Replication Flow for the first time to perform the Initial Load

3.1 In DHADM, you can see that a new subscriber and buffer table are created in SAP S/4HANA for CDS Z_SALESORDER (check via T‑Codes DHADM and DHRDBMON). This occurs when you run the Replication Flow for the first time. Subscriber ID: C30227C32B841FE19EF81806E972D0BE; Producer Type: CDC; Consumer Type: APE (ABAP Pipeline Engine).

3.2 Master Loggining Table /1DH/ML000000024 and Subscriber Logging table /1DH/SL000000024 got created and can also be monitored in the Transaction DHCDCMON.

Master Logging Table: /1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>, It captures delta records from triggers as the single source of truth.

  • Structure: Includes the base table’s key fields and a sequence key (/1DH/SEQUENCE).
  • Quantity: One Master Logging Table per CDC mapping in the CDS view. For example, two CDC mappings generate two Master Logging Tables.

Subscriber Logging Table: /1DH/SL<XXXXXXXXX>, It is populated by the real‑time job /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB when the Master Logging Table has records and Mirrors the Master Logging Table but uses the base table’s key fields.

3.3 Resilient Data Buffer Table: /1DH/RDB0000HK: /1DH/RDB<XXXXX>, It is a transient staging framework / buffer table in the ABAP system that acts as a temporary staging area between S/4HANA and SAP Datasphere, ensuring deltas are safely staged, acknowledged, and only deleted after successful delivery. You can monitor them via transactions DHRDBMON (S/4HANA) or LTRDBMON (DMIS-based systems).

  • For each CDS view + replication flow combination, the ABAP Pipeline Engine/APRS creates one or more RDB tables as buffer objects.  One CDS view + one replication flow → one RDB table. Same CDS view + second replication flow → another RDB table for that flow.
  • The final structure matching the CDS view; populated by the real‑time job /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA and Mirrors the CDS view fields; /1DH/SEQUENCE serves as the key.
  • /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA joins the Subscriber Logging Table with the base table to fill the Buffer Table, ensuring only delta records are forwarded to the target.

3.4 As shown below, the Initial Load has completed, and the Replication Flow is retrying deltas every 5 minutes as configured.

 

3.5 In SAP Datasphere, preview the Z_SALESORDER table; the Change_Type = 'L' after initial load is completed and Change_Date was updated when the records were inserted.

 

4. Execute the Replication Flow: Delta Load with INSERT, UPDATE, MODIFY and DELETE Scenarios

4.1 Using the ABAP program, INSERT 2 new sales orders into ZVBAK. In SE16N (ZISDSALESORDER), you’ll see 12 records, and DHRDBMON shows 2 new entries as Current records.

 

 

Buffer Table /1DH/RDB0000HK-/1DH/OPERATION value is U means “UPSERT” (used for both INSERT and UPDATE operations in the RDB buffer)

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load frequency interval elapses.

You’ll notice that the two new orders (5011, 5012) have Change_Type = “A” (upsert: insert if not present, or update by primary key if it exists), whereas “L” denotes the initial load (records loaded during the first full load).

 

4.2 UPDATE 3 existing sales order: 5004, 5005,5006.

You’ll notice that for orders 5004, 5005, and 5006, Change_Type changed from “L” (Initial Load) to “A” (Upsert).

4.3 DELETE three existing orders: 5006, 5007, and 5008.

In the buffer table /1DH/RDB0000HK, the /1DH/OPERATION value is “D” for DELETE operations

In the SAP Datasphere local table Z_SALESORDER, deleted records appear with Change_Type = “D.” On the source side (SAP S/4HANA table ZVBAK and the CDS view Z_SALESORDER), those records were removed from the database. This confirms that Replication Flows with CDC in SAP Datasphere capture all changes, CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE.

5.High‑Level Flow for ABAP CDS View Replication into SAP Datasphere

On the first execution of a Replication Flow, the system creates the subscriber tables, RDB buffer tables, and logging tables. These tables are generated per object + replication flow combination.

The Initial Load transfers data from the source CDS view to SAP Datasphere, and the corresponding Change_Type = Lin the Datasphere local table.

For delta loads, CDC database triggers capture CRUD changes on the base table and write them to the Master Logging Table.

  • Master Logging Table: Stores delta records with /1DH/SEQUENCE as the key.
  • Observer Job ( /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB ) Moves entries from the Master Logging Table to the Subscriber Logging Table.
  • Subscriber Logging Table: Holds delta records keyed by the base table’s keys.
  • Push Delta Job ( /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA ) Joins subscriber logs with the base table to prepare records for extraction.
  • Buffer Table: Provides the final CDS-aligned structure for Datasphere to ingest into the target defined in the Replication Flow.

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load interval has elapsed.

After the initial load, for delta processing:

  • Create/Update operations are represented as Change_Type = A
  • Delete operations are represented as Change_Type = D

 

Conclusion:

Replication Flows with CDC provide a reliable way to move CDS-based data from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere with clear handling for initial loads, updates, inserts, and deletes. During the first run, the full dataset is loaded into the target, and during delta processing the runtime automatically captures source changes through triggers, logging tables, and the resilient data buffer before delivering them to Datasphere. In the target local table, the Change_Type values help validate the lifecycle of each record: L for initial load, A for upsert-style delta changes, and D for deletions. This makes it easier to monitor data movement end to end and confirm that the source and target remain synchronized.

 

References:

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE 
  2. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/replication-flow-blog-series-part-1-overvi... 
  3. https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/datasphere/best-practices-troubleshooting 
  4. https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4428871866.html 

Happy Learning!😊

entry.title: Building a Conversational AI Agent in S/4HANA — Without Leaving the ABAP Stack
entry.content:
An experiment with two things at once: the yaai open-source ABAP addon and SAP AI Core as the LLM provider. The goal was to see whether a non-coder could wire an LLM directly into SAP S/4HANA — no external APIs, no middleware, and no writing a single line of code.

How This Started

I had two things I wanted to experiment with:

  1. yaai — an open-source ABAP addon by Christian Jianelli that lets you call LLMs from ABAP and register ABAP methods as tools a model can invoke. I wanted to understand what this actually meant in practice, not in theory.
  2. SAP AI Core — SAP's managed LLM platform on BTP. I had access to a running deployment and wanted to see how it behaved as the backend for an ABAP-native agent.

For the business scenario, I needed something simple — not because the scenario mattered, but because I wanted the complexity to come from the integration, not the domain. Batch management in SAP S/4HANA was the first thing that came to mind: logistics personnel ask the same questions every day (What's the shelf life expiration date on this batch? Which batches are about to expire?), the answers are all in standard SAP S/4HANA, and the data model is well understood.

All the ABAP code was written using Claude for Coding inside Visual Studio Code. I described what I needed, reviewed what came back, tested it in the SAP system, and fed errors back in. I did not write a single line of code. All this in less than a day!! My contribution was knowing what I wanted, understanding SAP well enough to validate the results, and a great deal of cutting and pasting.

All source code is available at github.com/davidlincourt-eng/yaai-batch-agent

What yaai Actually Is

yaai is a set of ABAP classes that lets you:

  • Call LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Ollama, and SAP AI Core) from ABAP
  • Register any ABAP instance method as a "tool" the model can invoke during a conversation
  • Maintain multi-turn conversation history
  • Use system instructions to give the model a persona and scope
  • Run agents with tool calling through a UI

It comes with a companion application, yaai_cockpit, which is a SAPUI5 app you deploy to your SAP S/4HANA system. It gives you a browser-based UI for configuring agents, registering tools, uploading prompt documents, and chatting with the agent — all without touching code.

Architecture in One Diagram

User (browser)
     │
     ▼
yaai_cockpit (SAPUI5, served from SAP S/4HANA)
     │  /sap/yaai/agent  (ICF REST)
     ▼
YCL_AAI_AGENT  ──► ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN  ──► SAP AI Core (gpt-4.1)
     │                  (OAuth2 token)              │
     │                                    tool call │
     ▼                                              ▼
ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS                      ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS_PROXY
(reads MCH1, MARA, INOB, AUSP)            (STRING → typed ABAP params)

Everything runs inside the SAP S/4HANA work process. The LLM decides what to ask; ABAP fetches the answer from the database; the cockpit shows the conversation.

The Key Advantage: No Published APIs

In a conventional integration architecture, making SAP S/4HANA batch data available to an AI application means leveraging the S/4HANA primary standard API for the Batch Master Record (OData service API_BATCH_SRV). It allows external systems to synchronously read, create, and change batch data an OData or REST API.

With the yaai approach, the LLM calls ABAP methods directly, inside the same SAP S/4HANA system. In this case, there is a standard API... but it's not aways the case. That means there is no API to publish, no external endpoint to secure, and no data ever leaves the system boundary to reach the model uncontrolled. The LLM receives only what the tool method returns — a formatted string — not raw table data.

This removes an entire layer of integration and keeps the data access logic in the one place where it has always been: ABAP.

An Important Caveat: Security

This experiment does not implement authorisation checks. The ABAP tool methods (ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS) read directly from the SAP S/4HANA database without verifying that the calling user has the appropriate roles or authorisations.

In a production scenario this is a significant gap. The tool classes should enforce the same authorisation checks that the equivalent SAP S/4HANA transactions enforce — using AUTHORITY-CHECK statements against the relevant authorisation objects (e.g. M_MSEG_WMB for batch display). Without this, any user who can access the cockpit chat can query any batch in any plant, regardless of their SAP role.

This is a proof of concept. Before deploying to a production or regulated SAP S/4HANA environment, authorisation checks must be added to every tool method.

What the Agent Can Answer

Once deployed, a user can ask questions in plain language:

User question Tool called What happens

"What is the SLED for batch 0000000045 of material TG22 in plant 1710?"GET_BATCH_HEADERReads MCH1 for VFDAT
"What classification characteristics does that batch have?"GET_BATCH_CLASSIFICATIONNavigates INOB → AUSP → CABN
"Which batches of TG22 expire in the next 30 days?"FIND_EXPIRING_BATCHESQueries MCH1 with date window
"List all expired batches for TG22 in plant 1710"FIND_EXPIRED_BATCHESFilters MCH1 where VFDAT < today
"Can you book a meeting for me?"—Refused (system instructions scope it)

Building It: The Five Moving Parts

1. The Connection Layer — ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN

SAP AI Core uses OAuth2 client credentials. Rather than storing a static API key, ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN fetches a fresh Bearer token from XSUAA on every call and injects the required AI-Resource-Group header. The token is valid for ~12 hours; since it's fetched per-call, there is no manual key rotation.

Credentials are stored in TVARVC (transaction STVARV) — never hardcoded. See Step 0 of the Setup Guide for the exact entries required.

2. The Tool Classes — ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS and its Proxy

The business logic reads from SAP S/4HANA tables:

" Read batch header — SLED lives in MCH1, not MCHA
SELECT SINGLE matnr, charg, vfdat, hsdat, lwedt
  FROM mch1
  INTO @DATA(ls_mch1)
  WHERE matnr = @i_matnr
    AND charg = @i_charg.
Note: VFDAT (shelf life expiration date) is stored in MCH1 (cross-plant batch table), not in MCHA (plant-level batch table). This is a common source of confusion — MCHA.VFDAT is always blank. Claude for Coding got this wrong on the first attempt and had to be corrected.

Because the LLM passes parameters as strings, a proxy class (ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS_PROXY) handles type conversion before delegating to the real class. This is a yaai pattern that prevents runtime conversion dumps when the model sends "30" where ABAP expects TYPE i.

The full source code for both classes is in the repository: ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS and ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS_PROXY.

3. The Agent Persona — System Instructions and Tool Guide

Two markdown files uploaded to the cockpit define how the agent behaves:

  • System instructions — sets the persona ("SAP S/4HANA Batch Management Assistant"), defines scope, and enforces formatting rules (dates as DD.MM.YYYY, "not maintained" for blank characteristics)
  • Tool usage guide — tells the LLM exactly which tool to call for which type of question, with a decision tree and parameter examples

This is the prompt engineering layer. The better these documents are, the more reliably the agent picks the right tool for a given question.

The two documents are in the repository: system instructions and tool usage guide.

4. The Cockpit — Configuring the Agent

The yaai_cockpit provides a browser UI for wiring everything together: registering the LLM API endpoint, defining tools, uploading documents, and creating the agent — all without touching code.

The complete step-by-step cockpit configuration — LLM API setup, tool registration, document upload, and agent creation — is covered in Steps 3 through 7 of the Setup Guide.

5. The Conversation

With everything wired up, the agent is accessible via the cockpit's built-in chat panel or through any custom UI that calls the /sap/yaai/agent ICF endpoint on the SAP S/4HANA system. To see the conversation in action, the project repository includes the full setup guide of the agent responding to batch management questions.

Here is an example of a conversation in the Cockpit

 

What Had to Be Fixed Along the Way

Getting yaai to work with SAP AI Core required a few patches to the yaai framework classes. These were not in the original yaai documentation — they came out of debugging, and Claude for Coding was essential for working through them:

Issue Fix

AI Core returns 400 for Anthropic model deploymentsUse a GPT deployment — SAP AI Core routes Anthropic and GPT through different API formats
"We're having a little trouble..." fallback from yaaiyaai defaults to the Responses API (/v1/responses); SAP AI Core only supports Chat Completions — add use_completions( abap_true )
Cockpit chat sends no response despite successful HTTP round-tripYCL_AAI_ASYNC_CHAT_OPENAI uses a static connection with no OAuth2 token and no AI-Resource-Group header — replace with ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN
STVARV uppercases credentialsEnable the Lowercase checkbox on each TVARVC entry — XSUAA credentials are case-sensitive
SLED always blank in responsesMCHA.VFDAT is empty; shelf life dates live in MCH1

Reflections on the Experiment

Two questions going in; two answers coming out.

Does yaai work? Yes. The tool calling pattern is solid, the cockpit makes agent configuration accessible without code, and the conversation history and system instructions work as advertised. The framework is genuinely usable.

Does SAP AI Core work as the backend? Yes, with caveats. It required patching two yaai framework classes and some non-obvious configuration (the use_completions flag, the AI-Resource-Group header, the OAuth2 token flow). None of these were documented anywhere — they came out of debugging. Once resolved, the integration was stable and the token refresh is fully automatic.

Does the "no coder" approach work? Also yes, with one important qualification. I wrote zero lines of ABAP. Every class, every method, every fix was generated by Claude for Coding in Visual Studio Code. My job was to:

  • Describe the SAP S/4HANA data model and what I wanted the agent to do
  • Paste the code into SE24 and SE38 and run the syntax check
  • Copy error messages back to Claude and iterate
  • Know enough about SAP to recognise when the output was wrong

That last point matters more than it sounds. The MCH1 vs MCHA issue — where shelf life dates are stored in the cross-plant batch table, not the plant-level one — was caught because I knew the data model well enough to question a blank SLED. Code generation quality was high enough that most errors were integration issues rather than logic errors. But you still need domain knowledge to validate the output.

The skill that mattered most was not coding. It was knowing SAP well enough to know what to ask for and whether the answer made sense.

What's Next

This experiment proves the pattern works. Natural extensions include:

Security hardening — AUTHORITY-CHECK in every tool method is the immediate priority before this goes anywhere near a real system.

More tools — The same pattern applies to stock quantities (MCHB), quality inspection lots (QALS), goods movements (MSEG), or any SAP S/4HANA data accessible from ABAP.

RAG integration — yaai supports Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Storage regulations, handling instructions, or compliance documents stored as markdown can be injected into the model context automatically.

Custom UI — The /sap/yaai/agent REST endpoint is available to any SAPUI5 or web application hosted on the SAP S/4HANA system. The cockpit chat is a reference implementation, not the only option.

Resources

  • yaai on GitHub — the open-source ABAP AI addon by Christian Jianelli
  • yaai_cockpit on GitHub — the management and chat UI
  • Full source code and setup guide — everything from this experiment, including certificates, ABAP classes, step-by-step instructions, and screenshots
  • SAP AI Core documentation — the BTP LLM platform used as the model provider

Thank You

A big thank you to Christian Jianelli for building yaai and yaai_cockpit and making them freely available. Christian also helped me directly with the initial setup of both libraries — without that support this experiment would have taken considerably longer to get off the ground. The yaai project is a genuine contribution to the ABAP community and I hope this post encourages others to experiment with it.

If you want to see where yaai is headed, Christian's latest blog post is a great read: yaai — ABAP AI Tools: Evolving Toward Agentic AI on the SAP Community.

entry.title: Product Cost Planning at a Reference Site in SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01
entry.content:
Simplifying Product Cost Planning for Retail and Fashion Organizations

Retail and fashion companies typically manage hundreds of stores, multiple distribution centers, and hundreds of thousands—or even millions—of articles. While the procurement and valuation processes across these locations are often identical, Product Cost Planning has traditionally been executed separately for every valuation area.
For organizations operating at this scale, this results in lengthy costing runs, increased database growth, unnecessary system processing, and additional maintenance effort.
To address this challenge, SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 introduces Product Cost Planning at a Reference Site, enabling organizations to calculate product costs once for a designated reference site and subsequently distribute the planned prices to operational sites.
This new capability significantly reduces processing effort while ensuring consistent product valuation across locations that share identical business characteristics.

Why Use a Reference Site for Product Cost Planning?
In many retail organizations, hundreds of DC's/stores purchase the same merchandise under identical procurement conditions.
Consider a typical scenario:
• Every DC/store purchases the same article (with different sizes) from the same vendor.
• Purchase prices are identical. usually at the generic article level.
• Inventory valuation follows the same costing logic.
Despite these similarities, traditional Product Cost Planning executes costing per variant article separately for every valuation area.
For an organization with 500 stores and 120,000 articles, this means generating approximately 60 million costing records, even though the calculated costs are often identical.
The result is longer batch runtimes, higher HANA resource consumption, and unnecessary duplication of data.
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, organizations can now calculate product costs once at a Reference Site and reuse those costs across all eligible receiving sites.
Why Use a Reference Site for Product Cost Planning?
Reference Sites have long been an integral part of SAP Retail. Organizations use them to centralize common master data, reducing maintenance effort and ensuring consistency across operational sites.
Historically, Reference Sites have supported:
• Article master maintenance
• Listing
• Assortments
• Purchasing information
• Pricing
• Logistics master data
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, SAP extends this proven concept to Product Cost Planning.
Rather than creating identical cost estimates for every receiving site, organizations can now:
1. Designate a Reference Site.
2. Perform Product Cost Planning only for that site.
3. Mark the resulting cost estimates.
4. Copy the future planned prices to assigned receiving sites.
5. Release the planned prices centrally.
The enhancement reduces redundant calculations while preserving consistent financial valuation.

How the New Solution Works
Instead of executing Product Cost Planning for every operational site, the calculation is performed once for the Reference Site. The resulting future prices are then distributed to all eligible receiving sites before being released as standard prices.
This reduces duplicated processing while maintaining a single, consistent standard cost across the landscape.

Prerequisites
A prerequisite for using the solution is that the Retail switch (ISR_RETAILSWITCH) is activated.
Using the new material price table, FMLT_PRICE, is optional. The solution functions effectively with or without it.

Step 1 – Assign the Reference Site

Transaction: REFSITE

Create the Reference Site and assign receiving plants.

Reference plant groupings can span multiple company codes, allowing centralized Product Cost Planning across organizational structures.

Step 2 – Edit the Selection List

Transaction: CKMATCON

Generate the material selection list used during Product Cost Planning. You can also implement BAdI BADI_RFM_PROD_COSTING_VARIANT to determine custom logic for identifying the reference material.

Step 3 – Generate the Reference Material Selection List

Transaction: S_ERX_29000720 - Create Selection List for Reference Site

Execute the report to generate a selection list containing only reference materials assigned to the Reference Site.

You can also implement BAdI BADI_RFM_PROD_COSTING_VARIANT to determine custom logic for identifying the reference material.

Step 4 – Execute the Costing Run

Transaction: CK40N

Create the costing run using the newly generated selection list.

Execute the costing process through the Mark step.

At this point, do not release the prices.

Proceed instead to copying future prices.

Step 5 – Copy Future Prices

Transaction: CK_COPY

This report distributes future planned prices from the Reference Site to all receiving plants across all active valuation views.

If company codes use different currencies, the system automatically performs currency conversion.

Two copy options are available:

  • Redistribute All – overwrite all existing planned prices.
  • Incremental Copy – copy only where planned prices do not already exist.
  • Prerequisites
    • Actual Cost Component Split settings must be consistent between reference and receiving plants.
    • Reference materials must be costed and marked for all active valuation views.

Step 6 – Release Future Prices

Transaction: CKME

Reports

  • SAPRCK23_JOB_SPLITTER
  • SAPRCKM_PRCH_JOB_SPLITTER

For large data volumes, SAP recommends using the wrapper reports to split processing into multiple packages, significantly improving release performance.

Example

Suppose an article has:

Cost Component

Value

Purchase Price

$12.00

Freight

$1.00

Handling

$0.50

Standard Cost

$13.50

Instead of calculating this cost estimate separately for every receiving distribution center:

  • DC1001 → $13.50
  • DC1002 → $13.50
  • DC1003 → $13.50
  • ...

SAP performs the calculation once:

Reference Site → $13.50

The planned price is then copied automatically to all eligible receiving sites.

Expected Performance Improvements

The primary objective of this enhancement is not to change the costing logic—it is to eliminate redundant calculations.

Typical improvements observed include:

Metric

Traditional

Reference Site

CK40N Runtime

High

Significantly Reduced

Cost Estimate Volume

Very High

Reduced

HANA Memory Usage

Higher

Lower

The exact improvement depends on:

  • Number of receiving Sites
  • Number of Articles
  • Procurement model
  • System landscape
  • Parallel processing configuration
Considerations Before Adoption
The underlying principle of this solution is simple: if the business conditions are the same, the cost estimate should also be the same.
Consider a retailer selling a T-shirt available in multiple colors but identical in size, procurement source, and purchasing conditions. Whether the T-shirt is gray or white, the calculated standard cost remains unchanged. Likewise, if all sites procure the article under the same commercial and valuation conditions, there is no business justification for generating separate cost estimates for every variant at every store.

Instead, SAP calculates the cost estimate once at the Reference Site and reuses it across all eligible receiving sites, eliminating redundant calculations while ensuring consistent product valuation.

 
Implementation guidelines
  • Group together sites with identical procurement characteristics.
  • Validate purchasing conditions before every costing cycle.
  • Schedule CK40N as a background job.
  • Regularly review cost component splits.
  • Monitor costing logs and exceptions.
Document the business rationale for using shared costing to support governance and audit requirements.

entry.title: Cross Docking Is Not Speed — It Is Controlled Movement
entry.content:
Cross Docking in SAP EWM: Why Synchronization Matters More Than Speed

Tags: SAP Extended Warehouse Management, SAP Transportation Management

Cross Docking in SAP EWM Why Synchronization Matters More Than Speed

Introduction

Cross Docking is often described as a warehouse process that moves inbound goods directly to outbound delivery without long-term storage. While this definition is technically correct, it can also be misleading.

During my study of warehouse execution scenarios in SAP EWM, I began asking a different question:

Is Cross Docking really about moving goods faster, or is it about synchronizing warehouse and transportation activities?

In real warehouse operations, moving a pallet from one location to another is rarely the biggest challenge. The real challenge is ensuring that the correct material reaches the correct outbound shipment at the correct time while maintaining operational stability.

From this perspective, Cross Docking becomes less about speed and more about controlled synchronization.

The Common Misunderstanding

Cross Docking is commonly associated with three benefits:

  • Reduced storage time
  • Faster material movement
  • Lower inventory handling

These benefits are real, but they only exist when warehouse execution and transportation execution remain synchronized.

Consider the following situations:

  • The outbound vehicle arrives later than planned.
  • A staging area has reached capacity.
  • Labour resources become unavailable.
  • Transportation priorities change after inbound goods have already arrived.

In these scenarios, simply moving material faster does not improve warehouse performance. Instead, uncontrolled movement may create additional handling, staging congestion, or incorrect loading sequences.

The objective therefore shifts from fast execution to controlled execution.

A Railway Station Analogy

A busy railway station provides a useful analogy.

Passengers arrive on one train and transfer to another. At first glance, this resembles Cross Docking because people move directly from one transportation flow to another.

However, when delays occur, passengers are not simply pushed onto the next platform.

Instead:

  • Waiting areas are used.
  • Platforms are reassigned.
  • Passenger flow is redirected.
  • Station operators continuously adjust movement until connections become synchronized again.

The railway station is not optimizing walking speed.

It is optimizing coordinated movement.

Warehouse Cross Docking operates in a very similar way.

How This Translates into SAP EWM

In SAP EWM, Cross Docking is supported by execution processes that connect inbound logistics with outbound requirements.

Depending on the business scenario, SAP EWM may use:

  • Warehouse Tasks (WTs) to move goods directly toward outbound staging.
  • Storage Types and Staging Areas for temporary buffering when direct movement is not possible.
  • Warehouse Process Types (WPTs) to control execution logic.
  • Door determination and staging processes to ensure the correct loading sequence.
  • Warehouse Monitor visibility to identify execution bottlenecks.

The objective is not simply to reduce storage time.

The objective is to execute warehouse movement without disrupting outbound commitments.

The Role of SAP TM

Cross Docking becomes significantly more effective when integrated with SAP Transportation Management (SAP TM).

SAP TM provides information such as:

  • Planned transportation routes
  • Vehicle arrival times (ETA)
  • Transportation Units
  • Freight Orders
  • Delivery priorities

This information allows warehouse execution to align with transportation planning before physical loading begins.

For example:

If SAP TM indicates that a vehicle will arrive two hours later than originally planned, immediately moving Cross Docking inventory to the outbound door may actually increase warehouse congestion.

Instead, SAP EWM can temporarily stage the material until transportation execution becomes synchronized again.

In this situation, synchronization creates greater operational value than immediate movement.

Temporary Staging Is Not a Failure

One misconception is that successful Cross Docking always means zero staging.

Operationally, this is not always true.

Temporary staging may be the correct business decision when:

  • Transportation schedules change.
  • Dock capacity becomes constrained.
  • Loading priorities change.
  • Labour availability decreases.
  • Multiple outbound shipments compete for the same resources.

SAP EWM provides flexibility through staging areas and storage types that allow warehouses to absorb operational variation while maintaining execution control.

Temporary staging is therefore not necessarily inefficient.

In many situations, it prevents warehouse disruption.

Visibility Drives Synchronization

Successful Cross Docking depends on continuous operational visibility.

Warehouse teams must understand:

  • Which inbound deliveries have arrived.
  • Which outbound deliveries require immediate execution.
  • Which Transportation Units are delayed.
  • Which staging locations are approaching capacity.
  • Which warehouse resources are available.

Without visibility, warehouse execution becomes reactive.

With visibility, warehouse execution becomes synchronized.

Business Value

When Cross Docking is viewed only as a speed optimization technique, warehouse teams may focus exclusively on reducing storage time.

When viewed as a synchronization strategy, different objectives emerge:

  • Reduce unnecessary handling.
  • Protect outbound service levels.
  • Improve dock utilization.
  • Align warehouse execution with transportation planning.
  • Maintain operational control during disruptions.

These outcomes provide greater long-term business value than simply moving goods faster.

Conclusion

Cross Docking should not be viewed simply as direct material movement.

Its real value lies in synchronizing warehouse execution with transportation execution while maintaining operational control.

SAP EWM provides the execution capabilities required to manage warehouse movement.

SAP TM contributes transportation planning and visibility.

Together, these systems enable coordinated execution rather than isolated movement.

In my view, the most successful Cross Docking operations are not those that move the fastest.

They are the ones that remain synchronized even when business conditions change.

Author: Rahul Kumar
Manyat Thinking

"Fast movement creates activity. Synchronized movement creates business value."

entry.title: Leading the Way for Post-Quantum Cryptography
entry.content:
By Sandip Dholakia, Chair Cryptography Innovation Hub, and Volkmar Lotz, Project Lead Crypto Agility Initiative, SAP


Quantum technologies are advancing rapidly, leading to a cryptographically relevant quantum computer that can break today’s public-key cryptography, which will be available sooner rather than later.

Like major industry peers, SAP is aware of the increased risk and is striving to be post-quantum ready by 2029 across its products, services and infrastructure. SAP has established a Cryptography Center or Excellence (CoE) that governs the migration process, maintains a cryptographic inventory, and coordinates migration projects across the company following a roadmap recognizing technical and business priorities for the identified cryptographic assets. Protecting customer data and privacy remains SAP’s highest priority, and to support this commitment, the SAP cryptography CoE has adopted a long-term strategy focused on cryptographic agility rather than one-off migration projects.

 

The Risk

Quantum computers can solve certain mathematical problems faster than classical computers. In 1994, Peter Shor demonstrated that a sufficiently powerful quantum computer could efficiently factor large integers and solve discrete logarithm problems. This breakthrough directly threatens widely used public-key cryptography algorithms, including RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) as their security relies on the difficulty of solving these problems with a classical computer. These algorithms form the foundation of many security mechanisms used across the industry, including within SAP products.


Why the Timeline Matters

Although the cybersecurity professionals understood the threat for decades, momentum accelerated after the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) proposed a timeline in 2024 to introduce Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) alongside traditional cryptography, starting in 2030 and fully phasing out traditional cryptography by 2035. This timeline is challenged by recent advances in quantum technologies: accelerated hardware development, improved quantum error correction and optimizations for Shor’s algorithm suggest that the gap between existing quantum computers and those needed to break public-key cryptography is closing.
